headline: Iban Customary Court Decisions Overturned 78% of the Time by Civil Courts
context: Legal Aid Foundation data shows civil courts overturned 78% of Iban native customary court decisions in Sarawak between 2018 and 2024, undermining indigenous judicial authority.

cards.big: Iban customary courts have existed for centuries.
cards.sub: Civil courts overturned 78% of their decisions in 6 years — treating customary law as subordinate to common law.

cards.big: The Native Courts Ordinance 1992 gives customary courts jurisdiction over adat matters.
cards.sub: But Section 28 allows any party to transfer proceedings to civil court, effectively creating an appeal to a different legal system.

cards.big: Civil court judges receive no training in customary law.
cards.sub: Decisions are overturned based on common law principles that may be irrelevant to customary context.

cards.big: New Zealand's Maori Land Court has exclusive jurisdiction over Maori land disputes.
cards.sub: Its decisions cannot be overturned by general courts. This preserves the integrity of indigenous law.

cards.big: A customary court whose decisions are overturned 78% of the time has jurisdiction in name only.
Customary courts need protected jurisdiction — areas where civil courts cannot intervene. Otherwise, indigenous legal systems exist only as long as no party objects.
